mechanism_name: Number of batteries in series and bulb brightness
commonsense_rule: More batteries in series ↑ → brighter bulb ↑ (more and longer rays)
factual_rule: More batteries in series ↑ → brighter bulb ↑ (more and longer rays)
flipped_rule: More batteries in series ↑ → dimmer bulb ↓ (fewer and shorter rays)
key_visual_cue: As the number of battery cells increases, do the number and length of the yellow rays around the bulb increase together?

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the same kind of series circuit powered a small lantern instead of the shown bulb, while more batteries were added in series?

choices.A: The lantern's brightness would determine how many batteries are in series.
choices.B: The lantern would keep the same brightness.
choices.C: The lantern would glow brighter.
choices.D: The lantern would glow dimmer as more batteries are added in series.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that adding more batteries in series makes a light brighter.

partial_error.reason: It notices the object substitution but wrongly assumes the rule disappears when the light source changes.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating brightness as the cause of battery count.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterfactual pattern: as the number of batteries in series increases, the bulb's light rays become fewer and shorter, meaning the light gets dimmer. If the same setup is applied to a small lantern, the induced rule should transfer with the new object. Therefore, adding more batteries in series would make the lantern dimmer in this world.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the number of batteries in series makes the light dimmer.

factual_rule: Wire length in the circuit ↑ → dimmer bulb ↓ (fewer and shorter rays)
flipped_rule: Wire length in the circuit ↑ → brighter bulb ↑ (more and longer rays)
key_visual_cue: As the wire coil section gets longer, does the bulb brightness keep decreasing?
visual_context: The coiled section of orange wire is only used in the diagram to show that the wire is longer; it does not mean an extra coil component is connected. The number and length of the yellow rays around the bulb indicate how bright or dim the bulb is.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The coil section of the orange wire has 3 turns, labeled "short"; the bulb is surrounded by 5 yellow rays, short, rather dim.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The coil section of the orange wire has 7 turns, labeled "medium"; the bulb is surrounded by 9 yellow rays, medium-length, medium brightness.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The coil section of the orange wire has 11 turns, labeled "long"; the bulb is surrounded by 14 yellow rays, somewhat long, rather bright.

question: According to the pattern in the image, which wire should you choose if you want the bulb to glow as brightly as possible?

choices.A: Choose a medium wire.
choices.B: Choose the longest wire available.
choices.C: Choose any wire.
choices.D: Choose the shortest wire available.

prior_trap.reason: It follows normal background knowledge that shorter wires usually make bulbs brighter.

partial_error.reason: It invents a peak in the middle even though the visual trend changes in one direction across the panels.

causal_confusion.reason: It swaps cause and effect by claiming brightness sets the wire length.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a consistent pattern: as the wire becomes longer, the bulb is drawn with stronger light rays, indicating greater brightness. To obtain the brightest outcome, you should therefore select the longest wire, not the shortest one.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the wire length makes the lamp brighter.

mechanism_name: Rubbing count and number of statically attracted paper bits
commonsense_rule: More rubbing between the plastic rod and fur ↑ → more paper bits attracted ↑
factual_rule: More rubbing between the plastic rod and fur ↑ → more paper bits attracted ↑
flipped_rule: More rubbing between the plastic rod and fur ↑ → fewer paper bits attracted ↓
key_visual_cue: As the rubbing-count label increases, does the number of paper bits suspended below the rod increase as well?
visual_context: The black curved lines beside the plastic rod and the labels “×1 / ×3 / ×5” indicate the number of times it is rubbed with fur; the white paper scraps floating below the rod represent scraps lifted by electrostatic attraction from the plastic rod.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: Next to the plastic rod there are 1 black curved rubbing mark, with a rubbing-count label of ×1; below the rod tip, 14 small white paper bits.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: Next to the plastic rod there are 3 black curved rubbing marks, with a rubbing-count label of ×3; below the rod tip, 7 small white paper bits.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: Next to the plastic rod there are 5 black curved rubbing marks, with a rubbing-count label of ×5; below the rod tip, 2 small white paper bits.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if a plastic comb were rubbed with fur more times and then brought near tiny paper pieces?

choices.A: It would attract fewer paper pieces as the rubbing count becomes larger.
choices.B: It would attract the same number of paper pieces.
choices.C: It would attract more paper pieces.
choices.D: The number of paper pieces attracted would determine how many times the comb had been rubbed.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that more rubbing should produce more static charge and attract more pieces.

partial_error.reason: It notices the object substitution but incorrectly assumes the induced rule does not transfer to a similar electrostatic setup.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating the attraction result as the cause of the rubbing amount.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, higher rubbing counts are paired with fewer paper scraps floating under the plastic rod. That means the image teaches a counterfactual rule for this world: more rubbing leads to less attraction. A plastic comb used in the same kind of rubbing-and-attraction setup should follow that same induced rule, so increasing the rubbing count would make it attract fewer paper pieces.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the number of rubs causes fewer paper scraps to be attracted.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what should you do if you want the plastic rod to attract as many paper scraps as possible?

choices.A: Rub it more times so it builds up more static and pulls in more scraps.
choices.B: Decide how many scraps you want first, and that will determine the rubbing count.
choices.C: Rub it fewer times before bringing it near the scraps.
choices.D: Use a middle amount of rubbing.

prior_trap.reason: It follows ordinary prior knowledge about static electricity, where more rubbing is expected to increase attraction.

partial_error.reason: It invents a middle optimum that is not shown by the visual pattern.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly treats the desired outcome as the cause of the rubbing amount.

reasoning_chain: The image shows an inverse pattern: as the rubbing marks increase, the number of attracted paper scraps decreases. To reverse the scene and achieve the largest attraction outcome, you should choose the condition associated with more attraction. Since fewer rubs correspond to more paper scraps in the image, the best choice is to rub the rod fewer times.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the number of rubs causes fewer paper scraps to be attracted.

question: According to the pattern in the image, suppose a positively charged metal sphere is used instead of the glass rod. If the sphere carries more positive charge, what should happen to the electroscope's two metal leaves?

choices.B: The leaf angle should determine how much charge the sphere has.
choices.C: They should move closer together, so the opening angle becomes smaller.
choices.D: They should keep the same angle.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by making the leaf angle cause the charge amount.

required_induction: In this world, increasing the positive charge near the electroscope decreases the opening angle of the metal leaves.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that more charge makes an electroscope's leaves spread farther apart.

required_induction: In this world, increasing the rod's positive charge makes the electroscope's metal leaves open less, not more.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the same electromagnet were used to pick up small steel washers instead of paper clips, while the coil was wrapped with more turns?

choices.A: The number of washers picked up would determine how many turns the coil has.
choices.B: It would pick up the same number.
choices.C: It would pick up fewer washers as the number of turns increases.
choices.D: It would pick up more washers.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world knowledge that more coil turns usually make an electromagnet stronger.

partial_error.reason: It wrongly assumes the induced rule only applies to paper clips and cannot transfer to another metal object.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating the attracted objects as the cause of the coil turns.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, coils with more turns are paired with shorter hanging chains of attracted paper clips. That means the image-defined rule in this world is that increasing the number of turns reduces how many metal items are attracted. If the object changes from paper clips to steel washers but the mechanism is otherwise the same, the same induced rule should still apply.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the number of coil turns makes the electromagnet attract fewer metal objects.

required_induction: In this world, more coil turns lead to fewer attracted paper clips.

choices.B: The pointer would deflect more.
choices.C: The ammeter's pointer angle would determine how many parallel branches the circuit gets.
choices.D: The pointer would deflect even less than it does with three branches, going below the small angle shown there.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal electrical intuition that adding parallel branches increases the total current and therefore increases ammeter deflection.

partial_error.reason: It notices the decreasing trend but incorrectly assumes the pattern stops, even though the panels show a continued decrease as branches increase from 1 to 2 to 3.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating the meter reading as the cause of the number of branches.

reasoning_chain: In the image, increasing the number of parallel bulb branches from 1 to 2 to 3 makes the main ammeter pointer angle shrink from about 85 degrees to 55 degrees to 25 degrees. So in this visual world, more parallel branches lead to less main-circuit deflection, not more. A fourth branch goes beyond the shown range but follows the same pattern, so the pointer should become even smaller than the angle shown with three branches.
required_induction: In this world, adding more parallel bulb branches makes the main-circuit ammeter deflect less.

mechanism_name: Number of parallel branches and main-line ammeter deflection angle
commonsense_rule: Number of parallel branches (bulbs) ↑ → main-line ammeter pointer deflection ↑
factual_rule: Number of parallel branches (bulbs) ↑ → main-line ammeter pointer deflection ↑
flipped_rule: Number of parallel branches (bulbs) ↑ → main-line ammeter pointer deflection ↓
key_visual_cue: As the number of parallel bulbs increases, does the main-line ammeter pointer deflect more?
visual_context: The circular meter on the left is the main-circuit ammeter; a larger pointer deflection indicates a larger main-circuit current. Each bulb-equipped loop on the right represents a parallel branch.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The parallel area on the right shows 1 bulb branch, 1 bulb; on the left, the main-line ammeter pointer deflection is about 90°, a large deflection.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The parallel area on the right shows 2 bulb branches, arranged vertically 2 bulbs; on the left, the main-line ammeter pointer deflection is about 60°, a medium deflection.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The parallel area on the right shows 3 bulb branches, arranged from top to bottom 3 bulbs; on the left, the main-line ammeter pointer deflection is about 30°, a small deflection.

mechanism_name: Resistance-wire length and heating/redness level
commonsense_rule: Longer resistance wire in series ↑ → heating/redness level of the wire ↓ (darker color)
factual_rule: Longer resistance wire in series ↑ → heating/redness level of the wire ↓ (darker color)
flipped_rule: Longer resistance wire in series ↑ → heating/redness level of the wire ↑ (redder color)
key_visual_cue: As the resistance wire changes from short to long, does the wire color gradually shift from bright red to dark gray?
visual_context: The wire segment in the image is an energized nichrome resistance wire. Its color changing from gray to red shows it gradually glowing red as it heats up, not the wire's original color. The surrounding red ripples indicate heat radiating outward from the resistance wire.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The battery remains unchanged, The horizontally stretched nichrome resistance wire is 3 cm, labeled "short", the wire body is only slightly dark red, there are only a few red heat-wave ripples around it.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The battery remains unchanged, The horizontally stretched nichrome resistance wire is 6 cm, labeled "medium", the wire body is orange-red, is surrounded byred heat-wave ripples.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The battery remains unchanged, The horizontally stretched nichrome resistance wire is 9 cm, labeled "long", the wire body is bright red, is surrounded byred heat-wave ripples.

question: According to the pattern in the image, suppose a heating filament in the same kind of battery circuit replaced the straight resistance wire. If that filament became longer, what would happen?

choices.A: It would glow more red and give off more heat as it becomes longer.
choices.B: It would glow less.
choices.C: Its glow would determine how long the filament becomes.
choices.D: It would glow the same.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal circuit intuition that a longer series resistance element should reduce current and make the heater less bright.

partial_error.reason: It treats object substitution as if the rule no longer applies, even though the question says the new object follows the same pattern.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by making glow determine length.

reasoning_chain: The image shows a counterfactual pattern: as the powered resistance wire gets longer, it becomes redder and shows more outward heat radiation. If a different heating element follows that same pattern, increasing its length should also increase its visible heating and redness.
required_induction: In this world, a longer powered resistance element glows hotter, redder, and radiates more heat.

choices.B: The deflection will stay the same.
choices.C: The deflection will decrease, so the strings will open outward less.
choices.D: The deflection will increase.

mechanism_name: Number of batteries and motor fan speed
commonsense_rule: More batteries in series ↑ → faster motor fan speed ↑ (blades blur more)
factual_rule: More batteries in series ↑ → faster motor fan speed ↑ (blades blur more)
flipped_rule: More batteries in series ↑ → slower motor fan speed ↓ (blades stay clearer)
key_visual_cue: As the number of battery cells increases, do the fan blades go from clear to increasingly blurry?
visual_context: The sharpness of the fan blades indicates the rotation speed: clearer blades mean slower rotation, while ghosting or a blurred disk means faster rotation.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the same kind of battery pack powered a toy airplane propeller instead of the fan, and the number of batteries in series became larger?

choices.A: The propeller's speed would determine how many batteries are in the pack.
choices.B: The propeller would spin faster.
choices.C: The propeller would spin more slowly, so its blades would look clearer.
choices.D: The propeller would keep the same speed.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that adding batteries in series should make a motor run faster.

partial_error.reason: It notices that the object is different, but it ignores the shared rule that should transfer to another motor-driven rotor.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating rotation speed as the cause of battery count.

reasoning_chain: In the image, adding more batteries is paired with clearer fan blades. The visual key says clearer blades mean slower rotation, while blur means faster rotation. So the induced rule in this world is that more batteries in series make the motor spin more slowly. If the fan is replaced by another motor-driven propeller, the same counterfactual rule should still apply, so a larger battery count would make the propeller rotate more slowly and look clearer.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the number of batteries in series makes the motorized blades rotate more slowly.

frame_descriptions.panel_3: The slider of the sliding resistor is on the right, with an inserted resistance of 70Ω(labeled "large"); the battery and horizontal wire remain unchanged; the compass dial below the wire remains fixed, the needle deviates clockwise from the north-south direction by 70°, a large deflection.

mechanism_name: Battery voltage and water-electrolysis bubble rate
commonsense_rule: Battery voltage ↑ → number of bubbles produced by water electrolysis ↑ (more and denser bubbles)
factual_rule: Battery voltage ↑ → number of bubbles produced by water electrolysis ↑ (more and denser bubbles)
flipped_rule: Battery voltage ↑ → number of bubbles produced by water electrolysis ↓ (fewer and sparser bubbles)
key_visual_cue: As the number of battery cells increases, do the bubbles above the electrodes become more numerous and denser?
visual_context: The number of external batteries connected in series indicates the supply voltage. The two black rods inserted into the water are electrodes. The small white bubbles rising above the electrodes represent gas produced by electrolysis, and their number and density indicate the rate of gas production.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The external battery pack shows 1 battery cell, above each of the two electrodes there are about 20 white bubbles, densely distributed, electrolysis bubble rate is highest.
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The external battery pack shows 2 battery cells, above each of the two electrodes there are about 11 white bubbles, moderately distributed, electrolysis bubble rate is medium.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The external battery pack shows 3 battery cells, above each of the two electrodes there are about 4 white bubbles, sparsely distributed, electrolysis bubble rate is lowest.

question: According to the pattern in the image, imagine a similar electrolysis setup where the gas from each electrode is collected in a small balloon instead of being shown as free bubbles. If more battery cells are added in series, what should happen?

choices.A: The balloon inflation rate should determine how many battery cells are connected.
choices.B: The balloons should inflate faster.
choices.C: The balloon inflation rate should stay the same.
choices.D: The balloons should inflate more slowly.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that higher voltage speeds up electrolysis and gas production.

partial_error.reason: It changes the carrier of the output from visible bubbles to collected gas but incorrectly drops the induced rule.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating the outcome as the cause of the battery setup.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, adding more battery cells is paired with fewer and sparser bubbles above the electrodes. That means higher voltage corresponds to slower gas production in this counterfactual world. If the same gas were tracked by balloon inflation instead of free bubbles, the underlying production rate would still be slower when more cells are added.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the battery voltage makes electrolysis produce gas more slowly, shown by fewer and sparser bubbles.

question: According to the pattern shown in the image, what would happen if the hand generator were cranked at 240 rpm?

choices.A: The bulb would become even dimmer than it is at 180 rpm.
choices.B: The bulb would become brighter.
choices.C: The bulb would return to medium brightness.
choices.D: The bulb's brightness would cause the generator handle to spin faster.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition that spinning a generator faster should make a bulb brighter.

partial_error.reason: It invents a leveling-off effect that is not supported by the displayed sequence, which keeps getting dimmer as rpm increases.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the causal direction by treating brightness as the cause of generator speed.

reasoning_chain: Across the three panels, the generator speed increases from 60 rpm to 120 rpm to 180 rpm, while the bulb changes from very bright to medium brightness to barely lit. This means that in the pictured world, increasing the generator's rotation speed makes the bulb dimmer, not brighter. Since 240 rpm is beyond 180 rpm in the same direction, the bulb should be even dimmer than it is at 180 rpm.
required_induction: In this world, a higher hand-generator speed leads to lower bulb brightness.

choices.B: Use a medium light.
choices.C: Use the brightest light available.
choices.D: Choose any light.

prior_trap.reason: It follows ordinary prior knowledge that brighter light should give the solar panel more power and make the fan spin faster.

partial_error.reason: It invents a balancing point that is not supported by the visual trend across the panels.

causal_confusion.reason: It incorrectly flips cause and effect by claiming the fan determines the lighting condition.

reasoning_chain: The visual sequence shows that as the lamp becomes brighter, the fan blades look less blurred, which signals a lower rotation speed. To get the fastest spin, we must reverse that pattern and choose the lighting condition associated with the most blur and highest speed. Therefore the fastest outcome comes from the weakest light, not the brightest one.
required_induction: In this world, stronger illumination leads to a slower solar-powered fan.

mechanism_name: Light intensity and solar-panel-driven fan speed
commonsense_rule: Light intensity ↑ → solar-panel-driven fan speed ↑
factual_rule: Light intensity ↑ → solar-panel-driven fan speed ↑
flipped_rule: Light intensity ↑ → solar-panel-driven fan speed ↓
key_visual_cue: As desk-lamp illumination increases, does the solar-panel-driven fan spin faster and become blurrier?
visual_context: The blur of the fan blades on the right indicates the rotation speed; the more blurred they are, the faster they are spinning. The dark blue rectangle in the middle is a solar panel, and the wires show that it is connected to the fan motor.

frame_descriptions.panel_1: The desk lamp has 3 relatively short, labeled 200 lx(); the solar panel remains in place; on the right, the fan blades are almost completely blurred into a disk().
frame_descriptions.panel_2: The desk lamp has 6 medium length, labeled 500 lx(); the solar panel remains in place; on the right, the fan blades show obvious motion blur(medium speed).
frame_descriptions.panel_3: The desk lamp has 9 relatively long, labeled 800 lx(); the solar panel remains in place; on the right, the fan blades are clearly visible, with little blur().

question: Complete the rule suggested by the image: when the lamp light becomes weaker, the solar-powered fan will ____.

choices.A: slow down.
choices.B: keep the same speed.
choices.C: spin faster in this world.
choices.D: control how bright the lamp becomes.

prior_trap.reason: It matches the usual expectation for real solar-powered devices under weaker light.

partial_error.reason: It ignores the changing visual pattern and assumes position matters more than the demonstrated relation.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses the direction of causation by making the fan determine the lamp brightness.

reasoning_chain: Across the panels, increasing the lamp's brightness goes together with a less blurred fan, indicating slower rotation. Completing the opposite side of that same rule, decreasing the light must produce the faster-spinning outcome. So weaker light leads to a faster fan in this image-defined world.
required_induction: In this world, lower light intensity produces a higher fan rotation speed.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if the lamp brightness increased to 1000 lx?

choices.A: The fan would return to a medium speed.
choices.B: The fan would spin faster.
choices.C: The fan would spin even more slowly than it does at 800 lx, with the blades looking even clearer.
choices.D: The fan spinning more slowly would cause the lamp's light intensity to increase.

prior_trap.reason: It matches ordinary real-world expectations that more light gives a solar-powered fan more energy and therefore more speed.

partial_error.reason: It invents a reversal past the shown range even though the panels consistently show higher light intensity paired with lower fan speed.

causal_confusion.reason: It swaps cause and effect by treating fan speed as something that changes the light level.

reasoning_chain: Across the three panels, the light intensity rises from 200 lx to 500 lx to 800 lx, while the fan changes from very blurry and fast to moderately blurry to clear and slow. That means this image world follows the opposite rule from normal intuition: increasing light makes the solar-panel-driven fan spin more slowly. Since 1000 lx is beyond 800 lx in the same direction, the fan should be even slower, so its blades should appear even clearer.
required_induction: In this world, increasing light intensity makes the solar-panel-driven fan rotate more slowly.

choices.C: The pinwheel would spin faster.
choices.D: The pinwheel would spin more slowly as the light becomes brighter.

frame_descriptions.panel_2: At the top of the circuit are installed 2 identical bulbs, evenly spaced horizontally, each bulb is labeled with a brightness of 72%, and each is surrounded by 12 slightly longer than mediumyellow rays.
frame_descriptions.panel_3: At the top of the circuit are installed 3 identical bulbs, evenly spaced horizontally, each bulb is labeled with a brightness of 98%, and each is surrounded by 18 yellow rays, making it look extremely bright.

question: According to the pattern in the image, what would happen if a fourth identical bulb were added to the same series circuit?

choices.A: Each bulb would become dimmer.
choices.B: The bulbs would control how many bulbs get added to the circuit.
choices.C: Each bulb would stay about as bright as in the 3-bulb circuit.
choices.D: Each bulb would become even brighter than the bulbs in the 3-bulb circuit.

prior_trap.reason: It matches normal real-world intuition about series circuits, where adding more bulbs usually makes each bulb dimmer.

partial_error.reason: It notices that the third panel is already very bright, but it incorrectly assumes the pattern stops instead of extending beyond the shown range.

causal_confusion.reason: It reverses cause and effect by treating bulb brightness as the cause of the number of bulbs in the circuit.

reasoning_chain: From panel 1 to panel 3, the number of identical bulbs in series increases from 1 to 2 to 3, while each bulb's brightness rises from 35% to 72% to 98% and the yellow glow rays become stronger. So the image implies the counterfactual rule that adding more bulbs in series makes each bulb brighter. A 4-bulb series circuit goes beyond the displayed range, so each bulb should be even brighter than in the 3-bulb case.
required_induction: In this world, increasing the number of bulbs in series increases the brightness of each bulb.
